ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roasted chicken and roast pork noodle; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish with meat — composite mixed dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this Singaporean roasted-meat noodle dish. A0CKP (composite mixed dish, noodle-based with meat) is the closest defensible analogous code in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Roasted Chicken and Roast Pork Noodle in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles topped with sliced roasted chicken, sliced siu yuk (roast pork belly), and seasoned with dark soy sauce and oyster sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken & Roast Pork Noodle — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Roasted Chicken & Roast Pork Noodle' (cr_id G00010708). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. The HPB record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which likely reflects the meat-and-sauce portion only and not the full dish with noodles; this is documented in notes. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous roasted pork and chicken composite data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; egg noodles (boiled) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this dish, which reflects the meat-dominant portion analysed. Because the actual dish contains egg noodles, a GI of 52 (low) is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled egg noodles (GI ~52, Atkinson et al. 2021). Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100. Since the HPB-reported carbohydrate for the analysed portion is 0.0 g, the GL per serving resolves to 0.0 for the meat-sauce portion as recorded; the actual GL of the full dish with noodles will be substantially higher and is noted as a clinical caveat.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium at 1050 mg/100g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods, driven by dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned noodle sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request minimal sauce, remove chicken and pork skin to reduce saturated fat, and limit portion to no more than 150 g to keep sodium intake below 1600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g reflects only the meat-and-sauce portion; the full dish with egg noodles carries a significant refined-carbohydrate load that will raise postprandial blood glucose, compounded by high sodium worsening insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion or substitute with whole-grain or low-GI noodles, remove chicken and pork skin to reduce saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance, and limit sauce to reduce sodium load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) combined with saturated fat from roasted pork belly skin and lard-tossed noodles elevates both blood pressure and LDL cholesterol, posing compounded cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request no lard in noodle tossing, remove all skin from chicken and pork, choose a smaller portion, and request reduced sauce to lower sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Roast pork (siu yuk) is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory cytokines; high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens RA-related joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute the pork component with additional chicken or fish, remove all skin to reduce AGE and saturated fat load, request less sauce to minimise sodium-driven joint inflammation, and avoid lard in noodle preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken and Roast Pork Noodle (烧鸡烧肉面, Shao Ji Shao Rou Mian) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker noodle dish featuring sliced Chinese-style roasted chicken and siu yuk (crispy roast pork belly) served over springy egg noodles tossed in a savoury sauce of dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and often lard. The dish is a staple at Chinese roasted-meat (siu mei) stalls found across Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops. The noodles are typically blanched and tossed dry or served with a small amount of broth on the side. The roasted meats contribute a rich, caramelised, slightly smoky flavour with a crispy pork skin texture contrasting the tender chicken. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records this dish at 171 kcal/100g with high protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate fat (10.5 g/100g). The carbohydrate figure of 0.0 g/100g in the HPB record reflects the meat-and-sauce portion analysed and does not represent the full dish with noodles, which carries a significant refined-carbohydrate load. Sodium is extremely high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is not halal due to the pork component. It is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is a culturally significant part of Singapore's Chinese hawker food heritage.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010708) with confidence 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data and documented. GI assigned by analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled egg noodles.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g and sugar_g as 0.0 g/100g. These are confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore Foodo database for the analysed portion of this dish, which reflects the meat-and-sauce component only. The actual full dish with egg noodles will contain significant carbohydrate; this is a known limitation of the HPB record and is documented as a clinical caveat in health_context. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB for the meat-dominant portion analysed. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1. These values are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2026, indicating limited published data for this traditional preparation. 3. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g of 3.2 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for analogous roasted chicken and roast pork belly composite (USDA SR Legacy entries for roasted chicken thigh and roasted pork belly), weighted approximately 60:40 chicken-to-pork ratio; this is an adapted estimate and not directly reported by HPB. 4. GI NOTE: The GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021). The HPB record carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g means the calculated GL per serving for the recorded portion is 0.0; the true GL of the full dish with noodles is substantially higher. 5. HALAL STATUS: This dish contains pork (siu yuk) and is not halal. 6. ONTOLOGY: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for this specific Singaporean roasted-meat noodle dish.
